claim: Hot context means context actively being used in a task (regardless of duration), while cold context is archived/historical context. Hot context can persist across long-running tasks and handoffs between agents.
actionable: When designing multi-agent systems with memory, distinguish between hot context (actively needed for the current task thread, may span hours/days) and cold context (archived learnings for future reference), and use different storage strategies for each.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Interactive agents serving end users need a deeper planning phase to one-shot correctly. The planning phase makes single-pass execution feasible even with strict latency constraints (a few minutes).
actionable: For latency-sensitive agent interactions, invest in comprehensive planning phases (schema modeling, context alignment, test definitions) upfront before execution to achieve correct one-shot behavior rather than iterative retries.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Sub-agents are valuable for parallelization and for handling side quests (tasks not on the critical path). Sub-agents should not require synchronous waiting unless truly sequential; otherwise orchestrate at a higher level.
actionable: When decomposing agent tasks, spin off sub-agents for parallelizable work and side tasks that may generate useful context. Only wait for sub-agent results if the main task is genuinely sequential.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Cumulate cold context (archived learnings, past skills, session histories) via background processes after task execution, not during. This keeps the agent focused and allows proper curation of what's worth archiving.
actionable: Implement background processes that post-process agent execution traces to extract and archive cold context (e.g., newly discovered patterns, skills, metadata) after the agent completes its work, rather than having the agent write to memory during execution.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Avoid graph databases for high write-throughput event systems; instead use data lakes (e.g., S3) for event storage and materialize custom graphs on demand. This is cheaper and faster for billions of daily events.
actionable: When building systems processing billions of events per day (change events, lifecycle updates), store raw events in object storage (S3) and construct specialized graphs on-demand rather than using graph databases which don't scale for write throughput.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Build organizational context layers in two phases: initial coarse pass to establish topology/ontology, then multiple refined passes on narrow questions to fill in confidence/derived attributes.
actionable: When building context graphs for enterprise environments, do a lossy first pass to establish entity relationships and structure, then run targeted agentic passes to compute derived attributes (e.g., 'is this internet exposed?') based on multiple data sources.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Sandbox isolation for agents should use defense-in-depth with multiple layers of permissioning at the box level; default to read-only and require explicit, deterministic elevation for write actions.
actionable: Design agent sandboxes with layered box-level permissions defaulting to read-only. Require agents to request elevated access through a policy engine for any write operations, ensuring all modifications are explicit and auditable.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Build trust with autonomous agent deployments via a ladder: start read-only (recommendations only), move to auto-routing, then auto-validation (testing in dev/staging), and only then consider production automation.
actionable: When deploying autonomous agents to customers, structure trust-building in stages: read-only recommendations → auto-routing for safe classes → validation in test environments → selective production automation for low-risk areas.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Use LLM judges with environmental parity to evaluate agent trajectories. At the simplest level use adversarial LLMs; at the most complex level, use LLMs that can replicate the agent's environment and reproduce outcomes.
actionable: Implement tiered LLM-as-judge evaluation: start with adversarial LLMs for simple checks, graduate to LLMs that can replicate the agent's execution environment and reproduce trajectories for comprehensive evaluation of long-running tasks.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Introduce a 'fail-safe recover' metric in evals: did the agent fail at a simple task and then recover successfully? High frequency of this correlates with prompt issues, not fundamental difficulty.
actionable: When designing eval metrics, include a 'fail-safe recover' signal that tracks whether agents fail on simple tasks then self-correct. A high rate of this metric indicates prompt quality issues rather than model capability limits.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Sample online evals rather than running 100% coverage. The goal is regression detection on a representative distribution. Use offline evals for the bulk of testing quality assurance.
actionable: For online evaluation in production, sample to a representative distribution rather than evaluating all traffic. Reserve offline evals for comprehensive quality assurance; use online evals primarily for drift detection and incident triggers.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: For enterprise environments with complex, custom systems, post-training open-source models on internal task-specific data often provides more value than using frontier models, especially when defensive patterns are highly customized.
actionable: When operating in domain-specific enterprise contexts (security, compliance, custom workflows), consider post-training open-source models on your organizational data (change control processes, remediation patterns) rather than relying solely on frontier models.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Defensive security is harder for AI than offensive security because defensive tasks are highly customized per organization, lack standardized datasets, and require understanding complex, heterogeneous environments.
actionable: When building AI for defensive/operational tasks, invest in context engineering and customization rather than expecting pre-trained models alone to work. Defensive problems lack the standardized labeled data that offensive tasks have.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Provide users real-time visibility into agent planning and execution trajectories in the UI. This enables users to catch mistakes, steer agents mid-execution, and builds appropriate trust and skepticism.
actionable: Design agent UIs to transparently show planning, reasoning, and tool choices in real-time. Allow users to interrupt or steer agents based on what they observe, fostering both trust and healthy skepticism.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Agents need deeper planning phases to 'one-shot' correctly, but it takes longer upfront. This tradeoff is worth it because correct one-shot execution in a few minutes beats multiple retry loops that take longer overall.
actionable: Accept that agent planning phases may add latency upfront (e.g., 1-2 minutes planning), because this enables correct single-pass execution and actually reduces total time vs. iterative retries.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Agent data analysis tasks require heavy lifting to handle massive scale (terabytes per customer). Performance-oriented system design is not optional for security and data-heavy use cases.
actionable: When agents operate on terabyte-scale datasets (security logs, vulnerability data, asset inventories), prioritize performance-first architecture from the start—don't retrofit performance later.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Agents operating on high-cardinality data need structured databases and indexes, not just files on disk. Large datasets (100M+ rows) can't live in context windows; provide agents with query tools.
actionable: For agents analyzing large datasets (millions of rows), combine file-based context for small working sets with database tools (SQL query access, indexes) to handle high-cardinality data analysis.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Coding agents and security/analysis agents use similar structural tools (bash, file systems, MCPs) but differ significantly in what correctness means and what domain knowledge they need.
actionable: Don't assume coding agent architectures directly transfer to non-coding domains. While tools (bash, files) may be similar, requirements for correctness, domain knowledge, and deployment validation differ fundamentally.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: Mean time to exploit (MTTE) for vulnerabilities has compressed from 2.5 years to minutes, driven by digital sprawl, increased software/vulnerabilities, and frontier models enabling novel zero-day discovery.
actionable: Understand that vulnerability remediation timelines have shifted from months to minutes. This justifies investment in autonomous systems and explains why human-only response is no longer viable.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)

claim: An agent's entry points (Slack, Teams, tickets, product UI) matter as much as the context layer itself. More entry points = more live, real-time data flowing into the system.
actionable: When building agent systems with context layers, invest in multiple integration points where users can provide feedback and context (Slack bots, ticket systems, native UI)—this keeps data live and real-time.
source_title: Inside Cogent's three-agent architecture for autonomous defense | Geng Sng (Co-founder, Cogent)
